title: SCUTELLARIA
description: Scutellaria (Chinese skullcap) is a medicinal herb containing bioactive flavonoids, primarily baicalin and baicalein, that modulate GABA-A receptors and exhibit anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and neuroprotective properties. It works by enhancing GABAergic neurotransmission, reducing neuroinflammation, and modulating stress hormone pathways, making it effective for anxiety, sleep disorders, and mood regulation.

research.title: Effects of a Scutellaria baicalensis/Crataegus laevigata, magnesium and chromium supplement on stressed individuals: A randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, crossover trial
research.summary: A 15-day trial showed Scutellaria baicalensis combined with magnesium and chromium significantly improved psychological well-being, cognitive performance, and sleep quality in stressed adults without serious adverse effects.

research.title: The Effects of Combined Scutellaria and Saffron Supplementation on Mood Regulation in Participants with Mild-to-Moderate Depressive Symptoms
research.summary: Combined Scutellaria and saffron supplementation demonstrated efficacy in improving mood regulation in participants with mild-to-moderate depression, with bioactive compounds modulating neurotransmitter systems and showing anti-inflammatory and anxiolytic effects.

research.title: Efficacy and Tolerability of a Chemically Characterized Scutellaria lateriflora Extract-Based Food Supplement for Sleep Management
research.summary: A randomized, double-blind crossover trial found Scutellaria lateriflora extract significantly improved sleep quality and showed good tolerability with no serious adverse effects reported.

research.title: The effect of scutellaria baicalensis and its active ingredients on depression: A systematic review and meta-analysis
research.summary: Meta-analysis of 13 preclinical studies showed Scutellaria baicalensis significantly reduced depressive behaviors through anti-inflammatory effects and modulation of neurotransmitter systems, though heterogeneity among studies was noted.

research.title: Pharmacological effects of baicalin in lung diseases
research.summary: Review of baicalin demonstrated therapeutic benefits in various lung diseases through antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, and antiviral effects, with applications in COPD, asthma, and pulmonary infections.

research.title: The effect of supplementation with Scutellaria baicalensis on hepatic function
research.summary: Systematic review found no evidence that Scutellaria baicalensis alone causes liver injury; previously reported cases involved concurrent use of other hepatotoxic supplements, suggesting the herb is safe for hepatic function when used appropriately.

research.title: Bioactive compounds from Chinese herbal plants for neurological health: mechanisms, pathways, and functional food applications
research.summary: Review highlighted Scutellaria baicalensis compounds demonstrate significant neuroprotective properties through anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and neurogenic effects, making them promising for prevention and treatment of neurological disorders.

sentiment.summary: Users consistently report effective anxiety reduction and improved sleep quality with Scutellaria, with many comparing it favorably to L-theanine or benzodiazepines. While some caution about potential memory effects at high doses, the majority find it well-tolerated and beneficial for stress management.

effects.label: Rapid Onset:
effects.description: Users typically report effects within the first dose when using tinctures or extracts, with benefits accumulating over 1-2 weeks of consistent use

effects.label: Individual Response:
effects.description: Effectiveness varies significantly between individuals, with some reporting strong benefits while others notice minimal effects

effects.label: Synergistic Effects:
effects.description: Many users find it works better when combined with magnesium, ashwagandha, or other GABAergic compounds rather than as a standalone supplement

effects.label: Formulation Matters:
effects.description: Tinctures and standardized extracts (particularly those with high baicalein content) appear more effective than powdered forms according to user reports

dosage.label: Standard Extract:
dosage.description: 256-400mg of Scutellaria baicalensis extract standardized to ≥98% baicalein taken 1-2 times daily is commonly reported

dosage.label: Tincture Form:
dosage.description: Users report effectiveness with tinctures at manufacturer-recommended doses, typically absorbed more quickly than capsules

dosage.label: Timing:
dosage.description: Most effective when taken 30-60 minutes before bed for sleep benefits, or during the day for anxiety without causing excessive sedation

dosage.label: Cycling:
dosage.description: Some users recommend cycling (5 days on, 2 days off) to maintain effectiveness and prevent tolerance development

sideEffects.label: Memory Effects:
sideEffects.description: One user reported concerns about potential memory lapses at higher doses (300-600mg), though causality was uncertain

sideEffects.label: Minimal Side Effects:
sideEffects.description: Most users report no significant adverse effects when using moderate doses of quality extracts

sideEffects.label: Sedation Risk:
sideEffects.description: Can cause oversedation if combined with other GABAergic compounds or taken at high doses

sideEffects.label: Hepatotoxicity Concerns:
sideEffects.description: Research suggests liver injury cases involved concurrent use of other hepatotoxic supplements, not Scutellaria alone

availability.label: Widely Available:
availability.description: Scutellaria supplements are readily available from various online retailers and health stores in both extract and tincture forms

availability.label: Quality Variance:
availability.description: Users emphasize the importance of choosing standardized extracts with verified baicalein content for consistent results

availability.label: Species Distinction:
availability.description: Both Scutellaria baicalensis (Chinese skullcap) and Scutellaria lateriflora (American skullcap) are available, with somewhat different applications but similar anxiolytic properties
